intro_text:
Dumas Beach is a dark-sand beach on the Arabian Sea, about 21 kilometers southwest of Surat in Gujarat, a strip roughly two kilometers long. In a country whose beaches are overwhelmingly golden, its sand is an unusual gray-black, and that single visual fact has given it a second, much louder identity: Dumas is routinely listed as one of the most haunted places in India.

The legend is tidy. The beach, the story goes, was once a Hindu cremation ground, and the dark color of the sand is the ash of the dead mixed into the shore. Visitors report whispers, giggles, barking dogs, an uneasy feeling after dark, and the usual catalog of people who supposedly wandered off and never came back. The reputation has been amplified by travel listicles and paranormal television until 'the haunted beach of Gujarat' is the first thing most Indians have heard about the place.

The daylight version is less dramatic and more honest. The dark sand has a geological explanation, not a supernatural one: it is colored by its mineral content, iron-rich sediment rather than ash. The cremation-ground claim is undocumented folklore, flagged as unverified even on Wikipedia, and the popular accounts themselves admit the stories are largely exaggerated to build tourist interest. And the beach is not an abandoned, fearful place. It is one of the most popular tourist beaches in South Gujarat, lined with food vendors and restaurants and busy with families, with the Dariya Ganesh temple sitting right beside the main beach.

That is the real character of Dumas: a beach that is a place of worship and a weekend picnic ground by day and a national ghost story by night, mostly in the retelling. The same dark sand that the legend reads as cremation ash is, in plainer terms, the seafront of one of India's densest industrial cities, Surat, the world's diamond-polishing capital, doing what an urban beach does, while carrying a reputation manufactured largely for the thrill of it.

It is a genuinely interesting beach, but not for the reason it is famous. The interest is in the gap: between the iron in the sand and the ash in the legend, between the Ganesh shrine and the ghost tour, between a city of six million's ordinary seaside escape and the most-haunted-beach-in-India branding that has been pasted over it.
honest_reckoning_note:
The honest problem with Dumas Beach is that its fame is essentially a marketing of fear, and it crowds out the real place. The dark sand is iron-rich sediment, a perfectly ordinary geological phenomenon; the claim that it is cremation ash is folklore with no documentation behind it, and even the travel sites that promote the haunting concede the stories are exaggerated to draw visitors. There is a small irony in a Ganesh temple, a place of active daily worship, sitting on a beach sold to the rest of the country as cursed ground. The 'most haunted beach in India' is, in daylight, a family picnic spot with food stalls.

The reckoning the ghost stories distract from is the mundane one. Dumas is the urban beach of Surat, a city of roughly six million and one of India's great industrial engines, textiles and the cutting of the large majority of the world's diamonds. It is a working coastline near the mouth of the Tapi, not a pristine one, and its real issues, like those of most Indian city beaches, are crowding, litter, and the water quality of an industrialized estuary, not the supernatural. The fairest thing to say is that the beach deserves better than its reputation: the legend is the least interesting true thing about it, and the most repeated.
reckoning_pullquote: The dark sand is iron, not ash; the scariest thing on the beach in daylight is the litter.

day_in_time.dawn: Early, the beach is calm and the dark sand reads simply as wet gray against a flat brown sea. A few worshippers come to the Dariya Ganesh temple beside the beach; the only sounds are the Arabian Sea and the temple bell. Nothing about the morning suggests the reputation.
day_in_time.midday: By midday it is a workaday Gujarat beach: hazy heat, the dark sand hot underfoot, food stalls firing up, families and day-trippers arriving from Surat. The water is shallow and brown, more for paddling than swimming. The 'haunted' branding evaporates entirely in the glare.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon is the busy hour, when the Surat weekend crowd peaks, the vendors do their trade, and the beach is at its most ordinary and most alive. The low sun does, briefly, make the black sand striking, the one moment the place looks as singular as its reputation claims.
day_in_time.night: After dark is where the legend lives. The crowds thin, the beach empties, the unlit dark sand and the sound of the sea do the rest, and the ghost stories take over the retelling far more than the place. It is, in fact, simply an unlit urban beach at night, which is reason enough not to wander it alone, no spirits required.

food_drink.name: Dumas beach food stalls
food_drink.description: The beach is lined with vendors and stalls serving Gujarati and Surti street food, the cheerful, crowded fairground eating that is the real daytime life of the place, in flat contradiction to its spooky reputation.
food_drink.where: Along the main Dumas beach front

food_drink.name: Surti specialties
food_drink.description: Surat's food culture is distinct and beloved, locho, khaman, ghari, undhiyu, and the city's famous street food, available in Surat and at and around the beach. Surtis are known across Gujarat for taking their food seriously.
food_drink.where: Surat city and the beach vendors

food_drink.name: Bring water; it's a day beach
food_drink.description: Dumas is a daytime picnic beach without resort infrastructure; come prepared with water and sun cover, eat from the stalls, and don't plan on a quiet sit-down meal on the sand.

timeline.title: Surat becomes a great port
timeline.description: The English East India Company establishes a trading factory at Surat in 1612, after the Battle of Swally, cementing the city's role as one of the Mughal empire's principal ports. Dumas is the seafront of this historic trading city.

timeline.title: Shivaji sacks Surat
timeline.description: The Maratha leader Shivaji raids and sacks the wealthy port of Surat in 1664, and again in 1670, episodes that mark the height and the vulnerability of the city's mercantile golden age.

timeline.title: The Surat plague and the clean-up
timeline.description: A pneumonic plague outbreak strikes Surat in September 1994. The crisis triggers a dramatic civic sanitation overhaul that transforms Surat over the following years into one of India's cleanest large cities, a striking reversal for an industrial boomtown.

timeline.title: The Tapi floods
timeline.description: In August 2006 the Tapi river, whose mouth lies near Dumas, floods catastrophically and inundates much of Surat, a reminder that this is a working, water-shaped industrial coast, not a resort one.

timeline.title: The 'most haunted beach' reputation spreads
timeline.description: Through the 2010s, travel listicles and paranormal television turn Dumas into a fixture on 'most haunted places in India' lists, attaching a national ghost-story brand to what is, by day, Surat's busy weekend picnic beach.
timeline.source: Indian travel media; Wikipedia: Dumas Beach

zones.name: The dark-sand strand
zones.character: The main beach: roughly two kilometers of unusually dark, iron-rich gray-black sand against the shallow brown Arabian Sea. The dark color is the beach's real signature and the seed of its entire legend.
zones.best_for: Seeing the rare black sand; the weekend crowd
zones.notes: The dark color is mineral/iron sediment, not, as legend has it, cremation ash.

zones.name: Dariya Ganesh temple
zones.character: The Ganesh temple sitting right beside the main beach, a place of active daily worship, a detail that sits oddly against the beach's reputation as cursed ground.
zones.best_for: The sacred, lived-in side of the beach
zones.notes: The shrine makes Dumas a devotional site by day and a ghost story by night, mostly in the retelling.

zones.name: The vendor front
zones.character: The crowded stretch of food stalls and vendors serving the Surat day-trip crowd, the cheerful fairground reality of one of South Gujarat's most popular beaches.
zones.best_for: Surti street food and the real, busy daytime atmosphere
zones.notes: This is what 'India's most haunted beach' actually looks like at 5 p.m.

landmarks.name: Dariya Ganesh Temple
landmarks.description: The Ganesh temple situated directly beside the main Dumas beach, an active place of worship and the beach's principal built landmark.

landmarks.name: Surat
landmarks.description: The city 21 km to the northeast whose beach Dumas effectively is: a historic Mughal port turned modern industrial powerhouse, the world's largest diamond-polishing center and a major textile hub of roughly six million people.

landmarks.name: Tapi river mouth
landmarks.description: The mouth of the Tapi river near Dumas, where Surat's river meets the Arabian Sea, shaping the sediment of the coast and the character of the water.

cultural_refs.title: India's 'most haunted beach' reputation
cultural_refs.description: Dumas's dominant cultural identity: a fixture of Indian 'most haunted places' lists and paranormal media, built on the dark sand and an undocumented cremation-ground legend, and, by the promoters' own admission, largely exaggerated.

cultural_refs.title: The cremation-ground legend
cultural_refs.description: The folk explanation that the beach was once a Hindu cremation ground and that its dark sand is the ash of the dead, an undocumented story that geology contradicts but that gives the beach its eerie brand.

cultural_refs.title: Surti food culture
cultural_refs.description: Surat's celebrated food, locho, khaman, ghari, undhiyu, spills onto the beach in its stalls; Surtis are proverbial across Gujarat for their devotion to eating well.

recurring_events.name: Weekend day-trip crowds
recurring_events.description: The reliable event at Dumas is the Surat weekend crowd: families, food stalls, and day-trippers filling the dark sand, especially in the late afternoon, the ordinary rhythm that the ghost reputation obscures.

things_to_know.label: It's a day beach, not a swim beach
things_to_know.note: The Arabian Sea here is shallow, brown, and not a swimming destination; Dumas is for the sand, the stalls, and the view, not for a serious dip. Treat it as a picnic and walking beach.

things_to_know.label: The 'haunted' reputation is overblown
things_to_know.note: The dark sand is iron-rich sediment, not ash, and the haunting stories are undocumented folklore amplified for tourism. There is no need for fear by day; the only real reason not to wander alone at night is that it is an unlit urban beach.

things_to_know.label: It's the beach of an industrial city
things_to_know.note: Dumas is the seafront of Surat, a dense industrial city near the Tapi's mouth. Expect crowds, litter on busy days, and the water quality of an urban estuary rather than a pristine shore.

things_to_know.label: Getting there
things_to_know.note: Surat International Airport (STV) is about 5 km away and the city of Surat about 21 km; Surat is a major railway and road hub. The beach is an easy short drive from the city.

access_note: Dumas Beach is about 21 km southwest of Surat in Gujarat, on the Arabian Sea. Surat International Airport (STV) is roughly 5 km away, and Surat, a major railway and road hub of about six million people, about 21 km northeast. It is an easy day trip by road from the city; there is no resort infrastructure on the beach itself.
spike_explainer:
Dumas Beach is famous for the wrong reason, and the right reason is more interesting than the wrong one. It is known across India as 'the haunted beach of Gujarat,' one of the most reliable entries on any 'most haunted places in India' list. The truth is a case study in how a single odd physical fact becomes a national ghost story, and what gets buried underneath it.

**The one real anomaly: the sand.** Indian beaches are, overwhelmingly, golden. Dumas, on the Arabian Sea about 21 kilometers southwest of Surat, is not: its roughly two-kilometer strand is an unusual gray-black. That is genuinely uncommon, and it is the entire seed of the legend. The dark color has a mundane, well-understood cause, mineral content, specifically iron-rich sediment, the same reason dark sands occur on coastlines around the world. It is geology, and nothing stranger.

**The legend built on it.** Folklore supplies a more dramatic explanation. The beach, the story goes, was once a Hindu cremation ground, and the black of the sand is the ash of the bodies burned there, mixed into the shore. From that premise the rest follows: the dead are said to haunt the place, and visitors report whispers, laughter, barking dogs, a feeling of being watched after dark, and the inevitable tales of people who walked onto the beach at night and were never seen again. None of it is documented. Wikipedia flags the burial-ground and curse claims as unverified, and, tellingly, even the travel sites that sell the haunting concede the stories are 'exaggerated and irrelevant' tales built to drum up interest among travelers. The haunting, in other words, is a tourism product, and a fairly transparent one.

**The daylight reality.** What the ghost brand obscures is that Dumas is not a lonely, dreaded place at all. It is one of the most popular tourist beaches in South Gujarat, lined with food vendors and restaurants, busy with families and day-trippers, and the Dariya Ganesh temple, a site of active daily worship, sits right beside the main beach. The same shore that the rest of the country files under 'cursed' is, locally, a Ganesh shrine and a weekend picnic ground. At five o'clock on a Sunday it is about as haunted as a food court.

**The city behind it.** And the legend buries a genuinely interesting context: Dumas is the urban beach of Surat. Surat was one of the Mughal empire's principal ports, where the English East India Company set up a factory in 1612 and which Shivaji sacked for its wealth in 1664. It survived a pneumonic plague in 1994 that triggered a civic clean-up so thorough it became one of India's cleanest large cities, and today it is the world's largest diamond-polishing center and a textile hub of around six million people, on the flood-prone mouth of the Tapi. Dumas is where that city goes to the sea. Its real issues are the real issues of an industrial-city beach, crowding, litter, the water quality of an urbanized estuary, not the supernatural.

**The company it should keep.** There are beaches whose dark sand is genuinely the headline and honestly explained, Reynisfjara in Iceland and Punaluʻu in Hawaii, where the black is volcanic rock ground down by the sea and presented as the geology it is. Dumas belongs in that company: a dark-sand beach with a real, ordinary cause. Instead it was sorted into a lazier category, the haunted-tourism circuit, where the explanation is a ghost rather than a mineral. The sand is the same kind of fact in all three places; only at Dumas was it dressed up as something to fear.

**Why it matters.** Dumas earns a place not as a horror destination but as a small, clear lesson in how reputation works. Give a beach one true peculiarity, black sand, attach a tidy supernatural story, let travel media and paranormal TV repeat it, and the manufactured version overwrites the real one so completely that 'haunted' becomes the first and often only thing anyone knows. The honest version, an iron-dark beach, a Ganesh temple, a wall of food stalls, and the seafront of a remarkable trading city, is right there in daylight for anyone willing to ignore the ghost tour. The scariest thing on the sand is the litter, and the most interesting thing is everything the legend talks over.

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: ~21 km SW of Surat, Gujarat — Arabian Sea

key_facts.label: Reputation
key_facts.value: Listed as one of India's 'most haunted' beaches
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Dumas Beach; Indian travel media

key_facts.label: The legend
key_facts.value: Said (undocumented) to be a former Hindu cremation ground

key_facts.label: Reality
key_facts.value: Popular South Gujarat day beach; Dariya Ganesh temple on site

margin_notes.text: Dark beach sand comes from heavy, often iron-rich minerals in the sediment, an ordinary phenomenon found on coasts worldwide. At Dumas it is the literal basis of the cremation-ash legend, and it is just geology.
The cremation-ground story is undocumented; Wikipedia tags the burial and curse claims as unverified. Even the travel sites promoting the haunting admit the tales are exaggerated to attract visitors.

The Dariya Ganesh temple sits right on the main beach, a place of active daily worship. Dumas is a devotional and family picnic site by day for the people of Surat, whatever the rest of India has heard.

Surat was a principal Mughal port (English East India Company factory, 1612; sacked by Shivaji, 1664). After a 1994 plague it became one of India's cleanest big cities, and is now the world's largest diamond-polishing center.

Dumas sits near the mouth of the Tapi, which flooded Surat catastrophically in 2006. This is a working, water-shaped industrial coast; its real concerns are crowding, litter, and estuary water quality.
